excerpt: the devil came to him in the guise of a woman and through cunning deception caused him to contract marriage sealed only by Allah as witness, then revealed his true form
source_work: Kitab al-Yanabiʿ / al-Iftikhar
source_location_hint: themes of Iblis's deception of worshippers and trials of ascetics

excerpt: the pious worshipper, to prevent the ruler from developing regard for his asceticism, set forth elaborate food and ate publicly, then distributed it all to the poor after the ruler departed in disapproval
source_work: Rahat al-ʿAql / Masabih fi Ithbat al-Imama
source_location_hint: spiritual psychology and the dangers of reputation

excerpt: a worshipper who saw a woman, recognized Satan's manipulation of his desire, sealed his sanctuary with stones, and spent years in mortification, saying that fullness of the belly caused his stumbling
source_work: Diwan al-Muʾayyad / al-Majalis al-Muʾayyadiyya
source_location_hint: spiritual struggle and self-discipline narratives

excerpt: the innocent worshipper thrust his left hand into fire and placed his right upon the woman's back without crying out, enduring the trial in silence until his hand swelled from shoulder to chest
source_work: al-Iqtisar / Daʿaʾim al-Islam
source_location_hint: trials of the righteous and divine justice

excerpt: Moses sheltered a white bird from a hawk, sacrificing his eye rather than abandoning the guest who sought refuge with him, fulfilling the divine commandment regarding the rights of one's neighbour
source_work: Ithbat al-Nubuwwat / al-Mashʾalah
source_location_hint: prophetic exemplars and neighbour-rights

excerpt: the man gave daily charity which postponed his death and hid him from Moses for twenty-four years, until he forgot his alms one morning, when he was immediately visible and killed according to Allah's command
source_work: Rahat al-ʿAql
source_location_hint: efficacy of charity and divine decree

excerpt: the believing woman abandoned her bread and child to prayer; when she returned, the child sat unburned upon hot coals and the bread was golden and perfect; her husband witnessed a second miracle when she sat in a hot oven and fragrant plants grew to her shoulders
source_work: Diwan al-Muʾayyad
source_location_hint: devotion and divine protection narratives

excerpt: the man reasoning that wine was the least sin, drank three glasses and became so intoxicated that he committed all four evils—killing the child, burning the Qur'an, and committing adultery—demonstrating that wine is the mother of all evils
source_work: al-Masabih fi Ithbat al-Imama / Tanbih al-Hadi
source_location_hint: dangers of wine and progressive moral corruption

excerpt: the old man earning two barley loaves from morning's labour, preferring his earned bread to the prophet's wealth, and demonstrating through prayer that he could transform dust to gold and gold back to dust according to his preference for honest livelihood
source_work: Jamiʿ al-Hikmatayn
source_location_hint: preference for labour and virtue over wealth

excerpt: the seventy-year worshipper was ordered to preach to the king, flayed alive by a butcher, silenced by divine command when he cried out, and his skin was preserved by a hidden believer who prayed with it for rain during famines
source_work: al-Majalis al-Muʾayyadiyya
source_location_hint: martyrdom and the power of the righteous deceased

excerpt: the beggar prayed three prayers for the couple: forgiveness, Paradise, and long life; the second prayer was granted through Allah's erasing and writing in the Umm al-Kitab; the third added forty years to their lifespan through charity given to him
source_work: Kitab al-Yanabiʿ
source_location_hint: intercession and the Day of Increase (Yaum al-Mazid)

excerpt: the orphan's father was found perfectly preserved beneath an apple tree, having been murdered by the garden owner who usurped his land; David then ordered the garden owner killed and gave the garden to the orphan, confirming Allah helps the victim though late
source_work: Taʾwil al-Daʿaʾim
source_location_hint: divine justice and restitution

excerpt: the worshipper after seventy years in a cave, upon emerging and finding his need unmet, reproached himself for lack of sincere devotion; Allah sent word that this remorse pleased Him more than the seventy years of worship
source_work: Rahat al-ʿAql
source_location_hint: value of contrition and divine grace

excerpt: Three souls repented with humility, each praying with sincere petition—one invoking Allah's command not to turn away the poor, another seeking forgiveness for wrongdoing the soul, the third asking liberation from punishment. Allah heard their prayers and turned back the calamity.
source_work: Kashkole (Tales of the Children of Israel)
source_location_hint: Section on repentance and divine mercy

excerpt: A young man bled from fear of Hell's torments though he had never entered that place. Jesus taught his disciples that those who merely contemplate Hell's severity are moved to such distress—imagine then the agony of those who burn within it.
source_work: Kashkole (Tales of the Children of Israel)
source_location_hint: Section on fear of divine punishment

excerpt: Four thousand people stood in prayer at dawn. Allah commanded Gabriel to destroy the village because the righteous scholars had not forbidden the wicked from evil, making peace with transgression. Thus the righteous and wicked were destroyed together for this silence.
source_work: Kashkole (Tales of the Children of Israel)
source_location_hint: Section on neglecting to forbid evil

excerpt: An old man ceased laboring when hope was removed from his heart, sitting idle in despair. When hope was restored, he returned to work with vigor. Thus hope is the engine of human endeavor; without it, the world would collapse into ruin.
source_work: Kashkole (Tales of the Children of Israel)
source_location_hint: Section on hope and human effort

excerpt: A man vowed to give one-tenth in charity if Allah granted him trading capital. His vow was fulfilled, and wealth multiplied abundantly. When the Devil sought to break his covenant through temptation, the man chose death over abandoning his oath. An angel delivered him, saying Allah would increase his wealth in reward for faithfulness.
source_work: Kashkole (Tales of the Children of Israel)
source_location_hint: Section on fulfilling vows and covenants

excerpt: A man suspended in a dome for three hundred years attributed his exalted station to two deeds: absolute trust in Allah such that anxiety about sustenance never entered his heart, knowing that those who worry about provision do not truly trust; and never speaking ill of anyone in his life.
source_work: Kashkole (Tales of the Children of Israel)
source_authority: Ismaili teaching on trust and virtue
source_location_hint: Section on trust and abstention from backbiting

excerpt: Two angels revealed that Allah fulfills every desire of the unbeliever in this world without reckoning, but reserves the desires of believers for the Hereafter, that He might reward them many times over in that eternal abode.
source_work: Kashkole (Tales of the Children of Israel)
source_location_hint: Section on divine wisdom in worldly and heavenly rewards

excerpt: Moses witnessed an idol-worshipper granted abundant fish and angelic aid, while a believing worshipper received only one small fish that slipped away, and whose family was destroyed. Upon seeing the believer seated in Paradise with joy and the unbeliever tormented in Hell with serpents and scorpions, Moses understood that eternal consequences far outweigh temporal ease. He repented of his hasty judgment.
source_work: Kashkole (Tales of the Children of Israel)
source_location_hint: Section on divine judgment between believer and unbeliever in this and the next life

excerpt: A woman who committed adultery and murder sought repentance. Moses rejected her, but Allah revealed He had accepted her remorse and would forgive even a graver sinner. When Moses asked who could be graver, Allah said: 'Whoever willfully neglects his prayer.'
source_work: Kashkole (Tales of the Children of Israel)
source_location_hint: Section on sincere remorse and divine forgiveness

excerpt: David met a woodcutter living since the time of Yunus who labored once monthly, giving three parts of his earnings in charity and eating twice yearly, and forty worshippers dwelling two hundred years in a cave sustained by one miraculous loaf and cup. Both refused David's company, the first because he would become their neighbor in Paradise, the second because his words would distract them from remembrance of Allah.
source_work: Kashkole (Tales of the Children of Israel)
source_location_hint: Section on recognition of the inhabitants of Paradise

excerpt: A bribed judge declared a camel born from a cow, reversing nature itself for gold. A devoted worshipper exposed the falsity by observing that nature never changes its laws—cows bear only cows, not camels. The judge was shamed by this refutation of his corrupt judgment.
source_work: Kashkole (Tales of the Children of Israel)
source_authority: Ismaili teaching on divine nature and justice
source_location_hint: Section on the unchanging divine order

excerpt: A man who made a lustful gesture in the Sacred House was cursed by a woman. Later, he was falsely accused of theft and his hand was cut off. Reflecting, he understood that this punishment was the consequence of his earlier transgression. Thus every affliction comes from one's own deeds, as stated: 'Every soul is bound by what it has earned.'
source_work: Kashkole (Tales of the Children of Israel); Quranic verse
source_authority: Ismaili moral narrative and Quranic teaching
source_location_hint: Section on consequences of personal deeds

excerpt: A blacksmith preserved his hand from fire through abstaining from unlawful relations with a woman during famine. When she finally returned and he fed her for Allah's sake alone, she prayed, 'If this man speaks truthfully, forbid him the Fire.' Allah answered her prayer. A hadith affirms: 'Whoever has power over a woman and refrains out of fear of Allah, He shall protect him from great fear on the Day of Resurrection, forbid him Hell, and admit him to Paradise.'
source_work: Kashkole (Tales of the Children of Israel)
source_authority: Ismaili spiritual exemplar and hadith tradition
source_location_hint: Section on reward for refraining from the unlawful
